branchingScenario.title: BI11.1-2 | Organ Function tests and Hormones — Clinical Case

startScreen.startScreenTitle: Liver Function Tests, Thyroid Profile & Renal Markers
startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 48-year-old man with a history of heavy alcohol use presents with jaundice. Work through this case interpreting liver function tests, thyroid function, and renal markers.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly interpreted LFT patterns, thyroid function tests, and renal markers with strong biochemical reasoning.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review AST:ALT ratio significance, TSH-T4 feedback, and albumin half-life.
Revisit ALT as a hepatocellular marker, the AST:ALT ratio in alcoholic hepatitis, thyroid feedback loops, and creatinine as a GFR marker.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 48-year-old man presents with jaundice, spider naevi, and palmar erythema. He admits to consuming alcohol daily for 15 years. His LFT panel shows:

  • AST: 280 U/L (elevated)
  • ALT: 120 U/L (elevated)
  • AST:ALT ratio: >2:1
  • Albumin: 2.8 g/dL (low)

The most specific marker of hepatocellular injury is ALT (alanine aminotransferase), a cytoplasmic enzyme found almost exclusively in hepatocytes. An AST:ALT ratio greater than 2:1 suggests alcoholic hepatitis, because alcohol depletes pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6), which is needed for ALT synthesis.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's AST:ALT ratio is >2:1. Why does this pattern specifically suggest alcoholic hepatitis rather than viral hepatitis?

alternatives.text: Alcohol depletes pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6), which is required for ALT synthesis, so ALT is disproportionately low
Alcohol directly destroys ALT enzyme in the bloodstream

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: Alcohol does not destroy circulating ALT. The mechanism is that alcohol depletes pyridoxal phosphate (B6), a cofactor needed for ALT synthesis, causing disproportionately low ALT.

alternatives.text: Viral hepatitis increases ALT production while alcohol increases AST

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Both hepatitis types release AST and ALT. The key is that alcohol depletes pyridoxal phosphate (vitamin B6), required for ALT synthesis, causing the characteristic >2:1 AST:ALT ratio.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! B6 depletion reduces ALT synthesis, elevating the AST:ALT ratio.

Additional tests reveal:

  • TSH: 12 mIU/L (elevated) — Free T4: 0.5 ng/dL (low)
  • Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (elevated)
  • Albumin half-life: approximately 21 days

The thyroid results show primary hypothyroidism: TSH is elevated because free T4 is decreased, and the feedback loop from the thyroid to the anterior pituitary is disrupted.

Serum creatinine is the most commonly used marker of glomerular filtration rate, while albumin reflects hepatic synthetic function.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's albumin is low at 2.8 g/dL. What does low serum albumin indicate in the context of liver disease, and why does it take weeks to decline?

alternatives.text: It reflects impaired hepatic synthetic function; albumin has a long half-life of approximately 21 days
It indicates renal albumin loss; albumin drops rapidly in hours

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: While nephrotic syndrome causes albumin loss, in this liver disease context, low albumin reflects impaired hepatic synthesis. Its long half-life (~21 days) explains the gradual decline.

alternatives.text: It indicates malnutrition only; albumin is not made by the liver

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Albumin is synthesised primarily by the liver. Low albumin in liver disease indicates impaired hepatic synthetic function. Its half-life is approximately 21 days.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! Key biochemical principles:

  • ALT: Most specific hepatocellular injury marker (cytoplasmic enzyme)
  • AST:ALT >2:1: Suggests alcoholic hepatitis (B6 depletion reduces ALT synthesis)
  • Primary hypothyroidism: High TSH + low free T4 (disrupted thyroid-pituitary feedback)
  • Creatinine: Most common GFR marker
  • Albumin: Reflects hepatic synthetic function, half-life ~21 days
